query: Is Anaplasma phagocytophilum an obligate gram-negative intracellular bacterium

retrieved_docs.title: Anaplasma Phagocytophilum -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Anaplasma phagocytophilum is an obligate gram-negative, intracellular bacterium that causes an acute febrile illness known as anaplasmosis or human granulocytic anaplasmosis (HGA). The organism is genetically related to rickettsia and is transmitted by Ixodes scapularis in the northeast United States and by Ixodes pacificus in California. The vector also transmits other organisms responsible for diseases such as Lyme, babesiosis, ehrlichioses and Powassan encephalitis. Anaplasmosis was formerly named human granulocytic ehrlichiosis (HGE). In 2001, the organism was reassigned to the genus Anaplasma . Cases of anaplasmosis have been identified worldwide; in the United States, it is mostly reported in the upper Midwest and the Northeast. Disease activity has also been reported in Northern Europe and Southeast Asia. [1] [2] [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Anaplasma Phagocytophilum -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: An obligate gram-negative intracellular bacterium causes anaplasmosis. Ixodes ticks transmit Anaplasma phagocytophilum . The main reservoir is the white-footed mouse, Peromyscus leucopus; however, a broad range of wild and domestic mammals have been identified as reservoirs. [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Anaplasma Phagocytophilum -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Anaplasma phagocytophilum is a gram-negative intracellular bacteria that causes an acute febrile illness known as anaplasmosis or human granulocytic anaplasmosis (HGA). Anaplasmosis generally presents with nonspecific symptoms such as fever, chills, malaise, headache, and myalgia. On rare occasions, a rash may be present. Patients may also report nonspecific gastrointestinal or respiratory symptoms. A minority of patients will develop life-threatening complications. This activity examines when this condition should be considered on differential diagnosis and how to properly evaluate for it. This activity highlights the role of the interprofessional team in caring for patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Anaplasma Phagocytophilum -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Microscopic examination of peripheral blood smear shows characteristic intracytoplasmic aggregates in neutrophils called morulae. This finding is detected 25% to 75% of the time in patients that have not begun therapy. The sensitivity of peripheral smear for diagnosis is higher during the first week of infection.

retrieved_docs.title: Anaplasma Phagocytophilum -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the most common etiology of anaplasmosis. Describe the history and physical exam findings typically seen in patients with anaplasmosis. Summarize the treatment options available for anaplasmosis. Employ interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination to improve the management of anaplasmosis and improve outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Anaplasma Phagocytophilum -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The involvement of the central nervous system is rare in HGA, with meningoencephalitis occurring in 1% of the cases. However, manifestations of peripheral nervous system involvement include brachial plexopathy, cranial nerve palsies, demyelinating polyneuropathy, and bilateral facial nerve palsy. Neurologic function recovery may take several months. [7] [8] [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Anaplasma Phagocytophilum -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: In animal models, A. phagocytophilum infection-induced IFN-gamma production which also activates macrophages and increases inflammatory tissue injury. Further, Anaplasma also can inhibit the fusion of lysosomes with the cytoplasmic vacuoles and inhibiting signaling pathways responsible for respiratory burst. Other effects of Anaplasma include downregulation of phagocyte oxidase activity, delay in apoptosis, ineffective binding to and transmigration of activated endothelium, and inhibition of phagocytosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Anaplasma Phagocytophilum -- Review Questions

retrieved_docs.title: Anaplasma Phagocytophilum -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: A rare but severe complication of HGA is the development of hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis (HLH) due to activated macrophages and lymphocytes that lack normal downregulation, thus exacerbating the tissue damage resulting from excessive inflammation.

retrieved_docs.title: Anaplasma Phagocytophilum -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Differential diagnosis of anaplasmosis includes human monocytotropic ehrlichiosis (HME), Rocky Mountain spotted fever, relapsing fever, tularemia, Lyme disease, Colorado tick fever, and babesiosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Anaplasma Phagocytophilum -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Anaplasma phagocytophilum is an obligate intracellular bacteria that survives and propagates within the host cell and can evade neutrophil antimicrobial functions. A. phagocytophilum infection is acquired through a tick bite and disseminates to the bone marrow and spleen. A. phagocytophilum can selectively survive and multiply within cytoplasmic vacuoles of polymorphonuclear cells. It affects the progenitors of myeloid and monocytic lineages, and it is seen in neutrophils in peripheral blood and tissue. The presence of Anaplasma in neutrophils induces proinflammatory responses leading to neutrophil deactivation, and the release of cytokines promotes neutrophil degranulation. Interleukin-10, IL12, and IFN-gamma are among the cytokines released which contribute to continuous tissue injury. IFN-gamma is primarily produced by innate immune NK and NKT cells as well as CD8 T lymphocytes. The development of tissue injury prevents neutrophils from exerting effective antimicrobial response. These cytokine-driven mechanisms as a response to infection explain the clinical manifestations associated with human granulocytic anaplasmosis.  Clinical cases may present with fever, pancytopenia, liver dysfunction or more severe manifestations such as shock or organ failure.

retrieved_docs.title: Anaplasma Phagocytophilum -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Given the potential for life-threatening complications associated with anaplasmosis, therapy must be instituted as soon as possible if a patient presents with signs and symptoms compatible with infection and in the right epidemiologic setting. Therapy for presumptive diagnosis should be initiated while waiting for laboratory confirmation.

retrieved_docs.title: Anaplasma Phagocytophilum -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Anaplasmosis is diagnosed by culture, histopathology, PCR, or serology. The presence of intracytoplasmic aggregates of Anaplasma in peripheral blood neutrophils can be diagnostic in 20% to 80% of symptomatic patients. First-line treatment is doxycycline 100 mg orally, twice daily administered for 14 to 21 days or at least 3 days after defervescence.

retrieved_docs.title: Anaplasma Phagocytophilum -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Anaplasmosis can present with life-threatening complications and is best managed by an interprofessional team that consists of an infectious disease expert, internist, laboratory specialist, hematologist, nephrologist, and an intensivist. The patient must be treated as soon as the disorder is suspected.

retrieved_docs.title: Anaplasma Phagocytophilum -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Anaplasmosis generally presents with nonspecific symptoms such as fever, chills, malaise, headache, and myalgias. On rare occasions, a rash may be present. The patient may also report nonspecific gastrointestinal (GI) or respiratory symptoms. A low number of patients manifest with life-threatening complications. Immunosuppressed patients are more likely to get hospitalized and have a higher risk of suffering from severe complications. Occasionally, co-infection with other tick-borne organisms ( Anaplasma , Lyme, and Babesia ) occurs given that Ixodes ticks are the common vector.

retrieved_docs.title: Anaplasma Phagocytophilum -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Approximately one-third to one-half of symptomatic cases require hospitalizations due to the severe presentation, and 3% to 7% have life-threatening complications. These severe manifestations include respiratory insufficiency, septic shock, multiorgan failure, rhabdomyolysis, and opportunistic infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1329
retrieved_docs.content: Oxytetracyklin is an antibiotic that exerts a physiologic effect of decreasing mitosis, reflecting its ability to suppress cellular proliferation as part of its antimicrobial action. Meclocycline is mapped to oxytetracyklin, indicating that the two agents are pharmacologically related and may share similar mechanisms of action or therapeutic indications. In clinical practice, oxytetracyklin may be used to treat داء الريكتسيات (rickettsial disease), leveraging its antimicrobial properties and its capacity to inhibit mitotic processes in the targeted pathogens. This interconnected knowledge highlights how the relationship between oxytetracyklin and meclocycline informs drug selection, while the drug’s effect on decreased mitosis underpins its effectiveness in managing rickettsial infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 491
retrieved_docs.content: Oxytetracycline is a member of the tetracycline and derivatives class of topical antibiotics, which are categorized as antibiotics for topical use. This antimicrobial agent exerts a physiologic effect of decreasing mitosis, reflecting its ability to inhibit cellular proliferation of susceptible organisms. In clinical practice, oxytetracycline may be used to treat rickettsial disease (داء الريكتسيات). The broader group of tetracycline and derivatives, topical antibiotics, therefore encompasses agents like oxytetracycline that are employed topically to combat infections and possess the characteristic effect of reducing mitotic activity in target cells.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 269
retrieved_docs.content: Oxytetracycline (oxytetracyklin) is an antibacterial agent that exerts a physiologic effect of decreasing mitosis, reflecting its ability to inhibit cellular proliferation. Oxytetracycline is mapped to meclocycline, indicating that the two antibiotics are closely related or share similar pharmacologic properties. In clinical practice, oxytetracycline may be used to treat رداء الريكتسيات (rickettsial disease), demonstrating its utility against certain intracellular bacterial infections. Meclocycline is classified as a member of the antiinfectives for treatment of acne, a therapeutic group that also includes sulfacetamide, highlighting its role in dermatologic therapy. Together, these relationships illustrate that oxytetracycline and meclocycline belong to overlapping classes of anti‑infective drugs, with oxytetracycline possessing a specific mitosis‑inhibiting effect and efficacy against rickettsial infections, while meclocycline is recognized as an acne‑treating antiinfective alongside sulfacetamide.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 522
retrieved_docs.content: Oxytetracyklin is an antibiotic that exerts a physiologic effect of decreasing mitosis, reflecting its impact on cellular proliferation. Oxytetracyklin is also linked to meclocycline, indicating that the two agents share a mapped relationship and belong to a similar class of anti‑infective compounds. Both oxytetracyklin and meclocycline are used in the management of skin infections such as acne; they are considered members of the broader group of anti‑infectives for acne, a category that also includes sulfacetamid. In addition to its dermatologic applications, oxytetracyklin may be employed to treat داء الريكتسيات (rickettsial disease). Rickettsial disease is classified under the parent category “other infections; including parasitic,” situating it among infections that have a parasitic component. Together, these relationships highlight the overlapping therapeutic roles of oxytetracyklin, meclocycline, and sulfacetamid in acne treatment and their broader utility in addressing certain parasitic‑related infections such as rickettsial disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 731
retrieved_docs.content: Oxytetracycline exerts a physiologic effect of decreasing mitosis and is used to treat rickettsial disease, which is classified under the broader category of other infections that include parasitic infections. Meclocycline is closely related to oxytetracycline and, like erythromycin and sulfacetamide, belongs to the group of anti‑infectives employed for acne treatment. Dihydroergotamine is contraindicated when combined with either erythromycin or posaconazole, indicating a significant drug‑interaction risk. Posaconazole is identified as a trypanocidal agent, and its use together with dihydroergotamine should be avoided. This interconnected knowledge underscores the antimicrobial roles of oxytetracycline, meclocycline, erythromycin, and sulfacetamide in dermatologic and infectious settings, the specific therapeutic application of oxytetracycline for rickettsial disease, and the important contraindications involving dihydroergotamine with erythromycin and posaconazole.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 1129
retrieved_docs.content: The antibacterial therapeutic role is fulfilled by both ciprofloxacin and paromomycin, indicating that each of these agents is employed for treating bacterial infections. However, the effectiveness of ciprofloxacin can be compromised when a patient is concurrently affected by عدوى وحيدات الخلية (single‑celled organism infection), suggesting that such co‑infections may diminish the drug’s antibacterial activity. This relationship underscores the need for clinicians to assess for protozoal or other single‑celled infections before or during ciprofloxacin therapy, while paromomycin remains a viable antibacterial option without the same reported limitation.
